title: Tristram of Lyonesse - VI - Joyous Gard
content:
В В В В A little time, O Love, a little light,
В В В В A little hour for ease before the night.
В В В В Sweet Love, that art so bitter; foolish Love,
В В В В Whom wise men know for wiser, and thy dove
В В В В More subtle than the serpent; for thy sake
В В В В These pray thee for a little beam to break,
В В В В A little grace to help them, lest men think
В В В В Thy servants have but hours like tears to drink.
В В В В O Love, a little comfort, lest they fear
В В В В To serve as these have served thee who stand here.

В В В В For these are thine, thy servants these, that stand
В В В В Here nigh the limit of the wild north land,
В В В В At margin of the grey great eastern sea,
В В В В Dense-islanded with peaks and reefs, that see
В В В В No life but of the fleet wings fair and free
В В В В Which cleave the mist and sunlight all day long
В В В В With sleepless flight and cries more glad than song.
В В В В Strange ways of life have led them hither, here
В В В В To win fleet respite from desire and fear
В В В В With armistice from sorrow; strange and sweet
В В В В Ways trodden by forlorn and casual feet
В В В В Till kindlier chance woke toward them kindly will
В В В В In happier hearts of lovers, and their ill
В В В В Found rest, as healing surely might it not,
В В В В By gift and kingly grace of Launcelot
В В В В At gracious bidding given of Guenevere.
В В В В For in the trembling twilight of this year
В В В В Ere April sprang from hope to certitude
В В В В Two hearts of friends fast linked had fallen at feud
В В В В As they rode forth on hawking, by the sign
В В В В Which gave his new brideпїЅs brother Ganhardine
В В В В To know the truth of TristramпїЅs dealing, how
В В В В Faith kept of him against his marriage vow
В В В В Kept virginal his bride-bed night and morn;
В В В В Whereat, as wroth his blood should suffer scorn,
В В В В Came Ganhardine to Tristram, saying, пїЅBehold,
В В В В We have loved thee, and for love we have shown of old
В В В В Scorn hast thou shown us: wherefore is thy bride
В В В В Not thine indeed, a stranger at thy side,
В В В В Contemned? what evil hath she done, to be
В В В В Mocked with mouth-marriage and despised of thee,
В В В В Shamed, set at nought, rejected?пїЅ But there came
В В В В On TristramпїЅs brow and eye the shadow and flame
В В В В Confused of wrath and wonder, ere he spake,
В В В В Saying, пїЅHath she bid thee for thy sisterпїЅs sake
В В В В Plead with me, who believed of her in heart
В В В В More nobly than to deem such piteous part
В В В В Should find so fair a player? or whence hast thou
В В В В Of us this knowledge?пїЅ пїЅNay,пїЅ said he, пїЅbut now,
В В В В Riding beneath these whitethorns overhead,
В В В В There fell a flower into her girdlestead
В В В В Which laughing she shook out, and smiling said,
В В В В пїЅLo, what large leave the wind hath given this stray,
В В В В To lie more near my heart than till this day
В В В В Aught ever since my mother lulled me lay
В В В В Or even my lord came ever;пїЅ whence I wot
В В В В We are all thy scorn, a race regarded not
В В В В Nor held as worth communion of thine own,
В В В В Except in her be found some fault alone
В В В В To blemish our alliance.пїЅ Then replied
В В В В Tristram, пїЅNor blame nor scorn may touch my bride,
В В В В Albeit unknown of love she live, and be
В В В В Worth a man worthier than her love thought me.
В В В В Faith only, faith withheld me, faith forbade
В В В В The blameless grace wherewith loveпїЅs grace makes glad
В В В В All lives linked else in wedlock; not that less
В В В В I loved the sweet light of her loveliness,
В В В В But that my love toward faith was more: and thou,
В В В В Albeit thine heart be keen against me now,
В В В В Couldst thou behold my very lady, then
В В В В No more of thee than of all other men
В В В В Should this my faith be held a faithless fault.пїЅ
В В В В And ere that day their hawking came to halt,
В В В В Being sore of him entreated for a sign,
В В В В He sware to bring his brother Ganhardine
В В В В To sight of that strange Iseult: and thereon
В В В В Forth soon for Cornwall are these brethren gone,
В В В В Even to that royal pleasance where the hunt
В В В В Rang ever of old with TristramпїЅs horn in front
В В В В Blithe as the queenпїЅs horse bounded at his side:
В В В В And first of all her dames forth pranced in pride
В В В В That day before them, with a ringing rein
В В В В All golden-glad, the kingпїЅs false bride Brangwain,
В В В В The queenпїЅs true handmaid ever: and on her
В В В В Glancing, пїЅBe called for all time truth-teller,
В В В В O Tristram, of all true menпїЅs tongues alive,пїЅ
В В В В Quoth Ganhardine; пїЅfor may my soul so thrive
В В В В As yet mine eye drank never sight like this.пїЅ
В В В В пїЅAy?пїЅ Tristram said, пїЅand she thou lookпїЅst on is
В В В В So great in grace of goodliness, that thou
В В В В Hast less thought left of wrath against me now,
В В В В Seeing but my ladyпїЅs handmaid? Nay, behold;
В В В В SeeпїЅst thou no light more golden than of gold
В В В В Shine where she moves in midst of all, above
В В В В All, past all price or praise or prayer of love?
В В В В Lo, this is she.пїЅ But as one mazed with wine
В В В В Stood, stunned in spirit and stricken, Ganhardine,
В В В В And gazed out hard against them: and his heart
В В В В As with a sword was cloven, and rent apart
В В В В As with strong fangs of fire; and scarce he spake,
В В В В Saying how his life for even a handmaidпїЅs sake
В В В В Was made a flame within him. And the knight
В В В В Bade him, being known of none that stood in sight,
В В В В Bear to Brangwain his ring, that she unseen
В В В В Might give in token privily to the queen
В В В В And send swift word where under moon or sun
В В В В They twain might yet be no more twain but one.
В В В В And that same night, under the stars that rolled
В В В В Over their warm deep wildwood nights of old
В В В В Whose hours for grains of sand shed sparks of fire,
В В В В Such way was made anew for their desire
В В В В By secret wile of sickness feigned, to keep
В В В В The king far off her vigils or her sleep,
В В В В That in the queenпїЅs pavilion midway set
В В В В By glimmering moondawn were those lovers met,
В В В В And Ganhardine of Brangwain gat him grace.
В В В В And in some passionate soft interspace
В В В В Between two swells of passion, when their lips
В В В В Breathed, and made room for such brief speech as slips
В В В В From tongues athirst with draughts of amorous wine
В В В В That leaves them thirstier than the salt seaпїЅs brine,
В В В В Was counsel taken how to fly, and where
В В В В Find covert from the wild worldпїЅs ravening air
В В В В That hunts with storm the feet of nights and days
В В В В Through strange thwart lines of life and flowerless ways.
В В В В Then said Iseult: пїЅLo, now the chance is here
В В В В Foreshown me late by word of Guenevere,
В В В В To give me comfort of thy rumoured wrong,
В В В В My traitor Tristram, when report was strong
В В В В Of me forsaken and thine heart estranged:
В В В В Nor should her sweet soul toward me yet be changed
В В В В Nor all her love lie barren, if mine hand
В В В В Crave harvest of it from the flowering land.
В В В В See therefore if this counsel please thee not,
В В В В That we take horse in haste for Camelot
В В В В And seek that friendship of her plighted troth
В В В В Which love shall be full fain to lend, nor loth
В В В В Shall my love be to take it.пїЅ So next night
В В В В The multitudinous stars laughed round their flight,
В В В В Fulfilling far with laughter made of light
В В В В The encircling deeps of heaven: and in brief space
В В В В At Camelot their long love gat them grace
В В В В Of those fair twain whose heads menпїЅs praise impearled
В В В В As loveпїЅs two lordliest lovers in the world:
В В В В And thence as guests for harbourage past they forth
В В В В To win this noblest hold of all the north.
В В В В Far by wild ways and many days they rode,
В В В В Till clear across JuneпїЅs kingliest sunset glowed
В В В В The great round girth of goodly wall that showed
В В В В Where for one clear sweet seasonпїЅs length should be
В В В В Their place of strength to rest in, fain and free,
В В В В By the utmost margin of the loud lone sea.

В В В В And now, O Love, what comfort? God most high,
В В В В Whose life is as a flowerпїЅs to live and die,
В В В В Whose light is everlasting: Lord, whose breath
В В В В Speaks music through the deathless lips of death
В В В В Whereto timeпїЅs heart rings answer: Bard, whom time
В В В В Hears, and is vanquished with a wandering rhyme
В В В В That once thy lips made fragrant: Seer, whose sooth
В В В В Joy knows not well, but sorrow knows for truth,
В В В В Being priestess of thy soothsayings: Love, what grace
В В В В Shall these twain find at last before thy face?

В В В В This many a year they have served thee, and deserved,
В В В В If ever man might yet of all that served,
В В В В Since the first heartbeat bade the first manпїЅs knee
В В В В Bend, and his mouth take music, praising thee,
В В В В Some comfort; and some honey indeed of thine
В В В В Thou hast mixed for these with lifeпїЅs most bitter wine,
В В В В Commending to their passionate lips a draught
В В В В No deadlier than thy chosen of old have quaffed
В В В В And blessed thine hand, their cupbearerпїЅs: for not
В В В В On all men comes the grace that seals their lot
В В В В As holier in thy sight, for all these feuds
В В В В That rend it, than the light-souled multitudeпїЅs,
В В В В Nor thwarted of thine hand nor blessed; but these
В В В В Shall see no twilight, Love, nor fade at ease,
В В В В Grey-grown and careless of desired delight,
В В В В But lie down tired and sleep before the night.
В В В В These shall not live till time or change may chill
В В В В Or doubt divide or shame subdue their will,
В В В В Or fear or slow repentance work them wrong,
В В В В Or love die first: these shall not live so long.
В В В В Death shall not take them drained of dear true life
В В В В Already, sick or stagnant from the strife,
В В В В Quenched: not with dry-drawn veins and lingering breath
В В В В Shall these through crumbling hours crouch down to death.
В В В В Swift, with one strong clean leap, ere lifeпїЅs pulse tire,
В В В В Most like the leap of lions or of fire,
В В В В Sheer death shall bound upon them: one pang past,
В В В В The first keen sense of him shall be their last,
В В В В Their last shall be no sense of any fear,
В В В В More than their life had sense of anguish here.

В В В В Weeks and light months had fled at swallowпїЅs speed
В В В В Since here their first hour sowed for them the seed
В В В В Of many sweet as rest or hope could be;
В В В В Since on the blown beach of a glad new sea
В В В В Wherein strange rocks like fighting men stand scarred
В В В В They saw the strength and help of Joyous Gard.
В В В В Within the full deep glorious tower that stands
В В В В Between the wild sea and the broad wild lands
В В В В Love led and gave them quiet: and they drew
В В В В Life like a GodпїЅs life in each wind that blew,
В В В В And took their rest, and triumphed. Day by day
В В В В The mighty moorlands and the sea-walls grey,
В В В В The brown bright waters of green fells that sing
В В В В One song to rocks and flowers and birds on wing,
В В В В Beheld the joy and glory that they had,
В В В В Passing, and how the whole world made them glad,
В В В В And their great love was mixed with all things great,
В В В В As life being lovely, and yet being strong like fate.
В В В В For when the sun sprang on the sudden sea
В В В В Their eyes sprang eastward, and the day to be
В В В В Was lit in them untimely: such delight
В В В В They took yet of the clear cold breath and light
В В В В That goes before the morning, and such grace
В В В В Was deathless in them through their whole lifeпїЅs space
В В В В As dies in many with their dawn that dies
В В В В And leaves in pulseless hearts and flameless eyes
В В В В No light to lighten and no tear to weep
В В В В For youthпїЅs high joy that time has cast on sleep.
В В В В Yea, this old grace and height of joy they had,
В В В В To lose no jot of all that made them glad
В В В В And filled their springs of spirit with such fire
В В В В That all delight fed in them all desire;
В В В В And no whit less than in their first keen prime
В В В В The springпїЅs breath blew through all their summer time,
В В В В And in their skies would sunlike Love confuse
В В В В Clear April colours with hot August hues,
В В В В And in their hearts one light of sun and moon
В В В В Reigned, and the morning died not of the noon:
В В В В Such might of life was in them, and so high
В В В В Their heart of love rose higher than fate could fly.
В В В В And many a large delight of hawk and hound
В В В В The great glad land that knows no bourne or bound,
В В В В Save the windпїЅs own and the outer sea-bankпїЅs, gave
В В В В Their days for comfort; many a long blithe wave
В В В В Buoyed their blithe bark between the bare bald rocks,
В В В В Deep, steep, and still, save for the swift free flocks
В В В В Unshepherded, uncompassed, unconfined,
В В В В That when blown foam keeps all the loud air blind
В В В В Mix with the windпїЅs their triumph, and partake
В В В В The joy of blasts that ravin, waves that break,
В В В В All round and all below their mustering wings,
В В В В A clanging cloud that round the cliffпїЅs edge clings
В В В В On each bleak bluff breaking the strenuous tides
В В В В That rings reverberate mirth when storm bestrides
В В В В The subject night in thunder: many a noon
В В В В They took the moorlandпїЅs or the bright seaпїЅs boon
В В В В With all their hearts into their spirit of sense,
В В В В Rejoicing, where the sudden dells grew dense
В В В В With sharp thick flight of hillside birds, or where
В В В В On some strait rockпїЅs ledge in the intense mute air
В В В В Erect against the cliffпїЅs sheer sunlit white
В В В В Blue as the clear north heaven, clothed warm with light,
В В В В Stood neck to bended neck and wing to wing
В В В В With heads fast hidden under, close as cling
В В В В Flowers on one flowering almond-branch in spring,
В В В В Three herons deep asleep against the sun,
В В В В Each with one bright foot downward poised, and one
В В В В Wing-hidden hard by the bright head, and all
В В В В Still as fair shapes fixed on some wondrous wall
В В В В Of minster-aisle or cloister-close or hall
В В В В To take even timeпїЅs eye prisoner with delight.
В В В В Or, satisfied with joy of sound and sight,
В В В В They sat and communed of things past: what state
В В В В King Arthur, yet unwarred upon by fate,
В В В В Held high in hall at Camelot, like one
В В В В Whose lordly life was as the mounting sun
В В В В That climbs and pauses on the point of noon,
В В В В Sovereign: how royal rang the tourneyпїЅs tune
В В В В Through TristramпїЅs three daysпїЅ triumph, spear to spear,
В В В В When Iseult shone enthroned by Guenevere,
В В В В Rose against rose, the highest adored on earth,
В В В В Imperial: yet with subtle notes of mirth
В В В В Would she bemock her praises, and bemoan
В В В В Her glory by that splendour overthrown
В В В В Which lightened from her sisterпїЅs eyes elate;
В В В В Saying how by night a little light seems great,
В В В В But less than least of all things, very nought,
В В В В When dawn undoes the web that darkness wrought;
В В В В How like a tower of ivory well designed
В В В В By subtlest hand subserving subtlest mind,
В В В В Ivory with flower of rose incarnadined
В В В В And kindling with some God therein revealed,
В В В В A light for grief to look on and be healed,
В В В В Stood Guenevere: and all beholding her
В В В В Were heartstruck even as earth at midsummer
В В В В With burning wonder, hardly to be borne.
В В В В So was that amorous glorious lady born,
В В В В A fiery memory for all storied years:
В В В В Nor might men call her sisters crowned her peers,
В В В В Her sister queens, put all by her to scorn:
В В В В She had such eyes as are not made to mourn;
В В В В But in her own a gleaming ghost of tears
В В В В Shone, and their glance was slower than GuenevereпїЅs,
В В В В And fitfuller with fancies grown of grief;
В В В В Shamed as a Mayflower shames an autumn leaf
В В В В Full well she wist it could not choose but be
В В В В If in that otherпїЅs eyeshot standing she
В В В В Should lift her looks up ever: wherewithal
В В В В Like fires whose light fills heaven with festival
В В В В Flamed her eyes full on TristramпїЅs; and he laughed
В В В В Answering, пїЅWhat wile of sweet child-hearted craft
В В В В That children forge for children, to beguile
В В В В Eyes known of them not witless of the wile
В В В В But fain to seem for sportпїЅs sake self-deceived,
В В В В Wilt thou find out now not to be believed?
В В В В Or how shall I trust more than ouphe or elf
В В В В Thy truth to me-ward, who beliest thyself?пїЅ
В В В В пїЅNor elf nor ouphe or aught of airier kind,пїЅ
В В В В Quoth she, пїЅthough made of moonbeams moist and blind,
В В В В Is light if weighed with manпїЅs winged weightless mind.
В В В В Though thou keep somewise troth with me, God wot,
В В В В When thou didst wed, I doubt, thou thoughtest not
В В В В So charily to keep it.пїЅ пїЅNay,пїЅ said he,
В В В В пїЅYet am not I rebukable by thee
В В В В As Launcelot, erring, held me ere he wist
В В В В No mouth save thine of mine was ever kissed
В В В В Save as a sisterпїЅs only, since we twain
В В В В Drank first the draught assigned our lips to drain
В В В В That Fate and Love with darkling hands commixt
В В В В Poured, and no power to part them came betwixt,
В В В В But eitherпїЅs will, howbeit they seem at strife,
В В В В Was toward us one, as death itself and life
В В В В Are one sole doom toward all men, nor may one
В В В В Behold not darkness, who beholds the sun.пїЅ

В В В В пїЅAh, then,пїЅ she said, пїЅwhat word is this men hear
В В В В Of Merlin, how some doom too strange to fear
В В В В Was cast but late about him oversea,
В В В В Sweet recreant, in thy bridal Brittany?
В В В В Is not his life sealed fast on him with sleep,
В В В В By witchcraft of his own and loveпїЅs, to keep
В В В В Till earth be fire and ashes?пїЅ

В В В В пїЅSurely,пїЅ said
В В В В Her lover, пїЅnot as one alive or dead
В В В В The great good wizard, well beloved and well
В В В В Predestinate of heaven that casts out hell
В В В В For guerdon gentler far than all menпїЅs fate,
В В В В Exempt alone of all predestinate,
В В В В Takes his strange rest at heart of slumberland,
В В В В More deep asleep in green Broceliande
В В В В Than shipwrecked sleepers in the soft green sea
В В В В Beneath the weight of wandering waves: but he
В В В В Hath for those roofing waters overhead
В В В В Above him always all the summer spread
В В В В Or all the winter wailing: or the sweet
В В В В Late leaves marked red with autumnпїЅs burning feet,
В В В В Or withered with his weeping, round the seer
В В В В Rain, and he sees not, nor may heed or hear
В В В В The witness of the winter: but in spring
В В В В He hears above him all the winds on wing
В В В В Through the blue dawn between the brightening boughs,
В В В В And on shut eyes and slumber-smitten brows
В В В В Feels ambient change in the air and strengthening sun,
В В В В And knows the soul that was his soul at one
В В В В With the ardent worldпїЅs, and in the spirit of earth
В В В В His spirit of life reborn to mightier birth
В В В В And mixed with things of elder life than ours;
В В В В With cries of birds, and kindling lamps of flowers,
В В В В And sweep and song of winds, and fruitful light
В В В В Of sunbeams, and the far faint breath of night,
В В В В And waves and woods at morning: and in all,
В В В В Soft as at noon the slow seaпїЅs rise and fall,
В В В В He hears in spirit a song that none but he
В В В В Hears from the mystic mouth of Nimue
В В В В Shed like a consecration; and his heart,
В В В В Hearing, is made for loveпїЅs sake as a part
В В В В Of that far singing, and the life thereof
В В В В Part of that life that feeds the world with love:
В В В В Yea, heart in heart is molten, hers and his,
В В В В Into the worldпїЅs heart and the soul that is
В В В В Beyond or sense or vision; and their breath
В В В В Stirs the soft springs of deathless life and death,
В В В В Death that bears life, and change that brings forth seed
В В В В Of life to death and death to life indeed,
В В В В As blood recircling through the unsounded veins
В В В В Of earth and heaven with all their joys and pains.
В В В В Ah, that when love shall laugh no more nor weep
В В В В We too, we too might hear that song and sleep!пїЅ

В В В В пїЅYea,пїЅ said Iseult, пїЅsome joy it were to be
В В В В Lost in the sunпїЅs light and the all-girdling sea,
В В В В Mixed with the winds and woodlands, and to bear
В В В В Part in the large life of the quickening air,
В В В В And the sweet earthпїЅs, our mother: yet to pass
В В В В More fleet than mirrored faces from the glass
В В В В Out of all pain and all delight, so far
В В В В That love should seem but as the furthest star
В В В В Sunk deep in trembling heaven, scarce seen or known,
В В В В As a dead moon forgotten, once that shone
В В В В Where now the sun shines, nay, not all things yet,
В В В В Not all things always, dying, would I forget.пїЅ

В В В В And Tristram answered amorously, and said:
В В В В пїЅO heart that here art mine, O heavenliest head
В В В В That ever took menпїЅs worship here, which art
В В В В Mine, how shall death put out the fire at heart,
В В В В Quench in menпїЅs eyes the headпїЅs remembered light,
В В В В That time shall set but higher in more menпїЅs sight?
В В В В Think thou not much to die one earthly day,
В В В В Being made not in their mould who pass away
В В В В Nor who shall pass for ever.пїЅ

В В В В пїЅAh,пїЅ she said,
В В В В пїЅWhat shall it profit me, being praised and dead?
В В В В What profit have the flowers of all menпїЅs praise?
В В В В What pleasure of our pleasure have the days
В В В В That pour on us delight of life and mirth?
В В В В What fruit of all our joy on earth has earth?
В В В В Nor am I, nay, my lover, am I one
В В В В To take such part in heavenпїЅs enkindling sun
В В В В And in the inviolate air and sacred sea
В В В В As clothes with grace that wondrous Nimue?
В В В В For all her works are bounties, all her deeds
В В В В Blessings; her days are scrolls wherein love reads
В В В В The record of his mercies; heaven above
В В В В Hath not more heavenly holiness of love
В В В В Than earth beneath, wherever pass or pause
В В В В Her feet that move not save by loveпїЅs own laws,
В В В В In gentleness of godlike wayfaring
В В В В To heal menпїЅs hearts as earth is healed by spring
В В В В Of all such woes as winter: what am I,
В В В В Love, that have strength but to desire and die,
В В В В That have but grace to love and do thee wrong,
В В В В What am I that my name should live so long,
В В В В Save as the star that crossed thy star-struck lot,
В В В В With hers whose light was life to Launcelot?
В В В В Life gave she him, and strength, and fame to be
В В В В For ever: I, what gift can I give thee?
В В В В Peril and sleepless watches, fearful breath
В В В В Of dread more bitter for my sake than death
В В В В When death came nigh to call me by my name,
В В В В Exile, rebuke, remorse, and, O, not shame.
В В В В Shame only, this I gave thee not, whom none
В В В В May give that worst thing ever, no, not one.
В В В В Of all that hate, all hateful hearts that see
В В В В Darkness for light and hate where love should be,
В В В В None for my shameпїЅs sake may speak shame of thee.пїЅ

В В В В And Tristram answering ere he kissed her smiled:
В В В В пїЅO very woman, god at once and child,
В В В В What ails thee to desire of me once more
В В В В The assurance that thou hadst in heart before?
В В В В For all this wild sweet waste of sweet vain breath,
В В В В Thou knowest I know thou hast given me life, not death.
В В В В The shadow of death, informed with shows of strife,
В В В В Was ere I won thee all I had of life.
В В В В Light war, light love, light living, dreams in sleep,
В В В В Joy slight and light, not glad enough to weep,
В В В В Filled up my foolish days with sound and shine,
В В В В Vision and gleam from strange menпїЅs cast on mine,
В В В В Reverberate light from eyes presaging thine
В В В В That shed but shadowy moonlight where thy face
В В В В Now sheds forth sunshine in the deep same place,
В В В В The deep live heart half dead and shallower then
В В В В Than summer fords which thwart not wandering men.
В В В В For how should I, signed sorrowпїЅs from my birth,
В В В В Kiss dumb the loud red laughing lips of mirth?
В В В В Or how, sealed thine to be, love less than heaven on earth?
В В В В My heart in me was held at restless rest,
В В В В Presageful of some prize beyond its quest,
В В В В Prophetic still with promise, fain to find the best.
В В В В For one was fond and one was blithe and one
В В В В Fairer than all save twain whose peers are none;
В В В В For third on earth is none that heaven hath seen
В В В В To stand with Guenevere beside my queen.
В В В В Not Nimue, girt with blessing as a guard:
В В В В Not the soft lures and laughters of Ettarde:
В В В В Not she, that splendour girdled round with gloom,
В В В В Crowned as with iron darkness of the tomb,
В В В В And clothed with clouding conscience of a monstrous doom,
В В В В Whose blind incestuous love brought forth a fire
В В В В To burn her ere it burn its darkling sire,
В В В В Her motherпїЅs son, King Arthur: yet but late
В В В В We saw pass by that fair live shadow of fate,
В В В В The queen Morgause of Orkney, like a dream
В В В В That scares the night when moon and starry beam
В В В В Sicken and swoon before some sorcererпїЅs eyes
В В В В Whose wordless charms defile the saintly skies,
В В В В Bright still with fire and pulse of blood and breath,
В В В В Whom her own sons have doomed for shame to death.пїЅ

В В В В пїЅDeath, yea,пїЅ quoth she, пїЅthere is not said or heard
В В В В So oft aloud on earth so sure a word.
В В В В Death, and again death, and for each that saith
В В В В Ten tongues chime answer to the sound of death.
В В В В Good end God send us ever, so men pray.
В В В В But I, this end God send me, would I say,
В В В В To die not of division and a heart
В В В В Rent or with sword of severance cloven apart,
В В В В But only when thou diest and only where thou art,
В В В В O thou my soul and spirit and breath to me,
В В В В O light, life, love! yea, let this only be,
В В В В That dying I may praise God who gave me thee,
В В В В Let hap what will thereafter.пїЅ

В В В В So that day
В В В В They communed, even till even was worn away,
В В В В Nor aught they said seemed strange or sad to say,
В В В В But sweet as nightпїЅs dim dawn to weariness.
В В В В Nor loved they life or love for deathпїЅs sake less,
В В В В Nor feared they death for loveпїЅs or lifeпїЅs sake more
В В В В And on the sounding soft funereal shore
В В В В They, watching till the day should wholly die,
В В В В Saw the far sea sweep to the far grey sky,
В В В В Saw the long sands sweep to the long grey sea.
В В В В And night made one sweet mist of moor and lea,
В В В В And only far off shore the foam gave light.
В В В В And life in them sank silent as the night.
meaning:
Algernon Charles Swinburne's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "Tristram of Lyonesse - VI - Joyous Gard"...
